coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue–green photoluminescent carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meng Li
Chen, Bin Bin
Liu, Ze Xi
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Though many methods of detecting 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) mainly have been developed recent years, quantification of TNP in environmental matrixes still faces up to great challenges because all the nitroaromatic explosives reveal highly similar chemical structure. In the present work, we have developed a selective and sensitive method for detection of TNP by amorphous photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), which are prepared through a simple hydrothermal route using spermine and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors. The as-prepared CNDs are found to show blue–green photoluminescence, excitation-wavelength independence, and excellent chemical and optical stability. Owing to the strong characteristic absorption of TNP at 356nm (ε=1.048×104 cm−1 M−1), which has a good spectral overlap with the excitation band of CNDs, the fluorescence intensity of CNDs at 490nm is linearly quenched with the adding concentration of TNP in the range of 0.1–100μM. The developing assay based on inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism for the detection of TNP is selective and convenient, showing that the as-prepared CNDs have applicable prospect in the concept of simplicity and specificity in analytical chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP)
Carbon nanodots (CNDs)
Hydrothermal route
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The stability of the as-prepared CNDs
Specific binding of the as-prepared CNDs with TNP
Determination of TNP in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All authors herein are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant no. 21535006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CNDs. (a) TEM image (inset: particle size distribution); (b) the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the CNDs. (Inset: CNDs solution under the 365nm UV lights lamp).
The elemental and structural analysis of the as-prepared CNDs. (a) FT-IR; (b) XPS; (c) C1s and (d) Raman spectra of the as-prepared CNDs.
The stability investigation of the as-prepared CNDs. (a) pH stability; (b) the stability in a salty medium; (c) the antioxidant capacity; (d) photostability of CNDs. λ ex: 360nm; c CNDs: 5μg/mL.

The fluorescence quenching of CNDs by TNP. (a) Fluorescence responses of the CNDs in the presence of different concentrations of TNP (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 750 and 1000μM). Inset: CNDs solution (left) without and (right) with 1mM TNP under the 365nm UV lights lamp; (b) the sensitivity of TNP detection. pH: 6.09; λ ex: 360nm; c CNDs: 5 μg/mL; I
0 and I are fluorescence intensity in absence and presence of TNP, respectively.

The detection mechanism of TNP. (a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of all nitroaromatic explosives and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CNDs; (b) fluorescence decay profiles of the CNDs in the absence and presence of TNP.

Interferences of (a) other nitroaromatics and (b) metal ions in BR buffer (pH 6.09). The columns in (b) represent: 1, control; 2, Cu2+; 3, Cr3+; 4, K+; 5, Ni2+; 6, Cd2+; 7, Al3+; 8, Hg2+; 9, Co2+; 10, Zn2+; 11, Pb2+; 12, Fe3+; 13, Na+; 14, Ca2+; 15, Mn2+; 16, Mg2+; 17, Ba2+. Concentrations of TNP, nitroaromatics and all the metal ions are 100µM. λ ex: 360nm; c CNDs: 5µg/mL.
Illustration of the synthesis process of CNDs from m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and spermine by a simple hydrothermal route and the highly selective and sensitive detection of TNP through IFE.

Determination of TNP in two water samples (Sample 1, running water; sample 2, lake water). pH: 6.09; λ ex: 360nm; c CNDs: 5 μg/mL. a Average of three replicate measurements. b Theoritical t-students test value=4.303 for n=3 (p=0.05), and the theoritical F-value=19.00 for n=3 (p=0.05).

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue–green photoluminescent carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Though many methods of detecting 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) mainly have been developed recent years, quantification of TNP in environmental matrixes still faces up to great challenges because all the nitroaromatic explosives reveal highly similar chemical structure. In the present work, we have developed a selective and sensitive method for detection of TNP by amorphous photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), which are prepared through a simple hydrothermal route using spermine and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors. The as-prepared CNDs are found to show blue–green photoluminescence, excitation-wavelength independence, and excellent chemical and optical stability. Owing to the strong characteristic absorption of TNP at 356nm (ε=1.048×104 cm−1 M−1), which has a good spectral overlap with the excitation band of CNDs, the fluorescence intensity of CNDs at 490nm is linearly quenched with the adding concentration of TNP in the range of 0.1–100μM. The developing assay based on inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism for the detection of TNP is selective and convenient, showing that the as-prepared CNDs have applicable prospect in the concept of simplicity and specificity in analytical chemistry.

Amorphous photoluminescent carbon nanodots with good stability were prepared for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP through strong inner filter effect.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP) is a very famous versatile industrial organic compound widely used as pesticides, pigments, dyes, pharmaceuticals and explosive materials [1–3]. But only a small dose of TNP in surrounding environments can directly contaminate the soil and aquatic systems [4–6]. In this regard, it shows great challenges to develop sensitive and selective measurement of TNP levels in environmental matrixes. Common TNP detection methods utilize mass spectrometry [7], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [8,9], field-effect transistors (FETs) [10], electrochemical methods [11] and fluorescence (FL) method [12–14], etc. Among these methods, FL sensing is a fast, simple and non-destructive operation method. In terms of FL detection of TNP, various mechanisms have been established including photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [13,15,16], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [12,17], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [5], strong electrostatic interactions (EI) [18] and inner filter effect (IFE) [19]. As all the nitroaromatic explosives are highly electron deficient in nature and own similar chemical structure, it is very difficult to distinguish and quantify one from a mixture of different nitroaromatic compounds by PET, FRET, EI and AIE mechanism. It is valuable for convenient synthesis fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of the aqueous contaminant TNP from other nitroaromatic explosives.

Numerous fluorescent probes have been developed to follow the PET, FRET, EI and AIE mechanism, such as reduced graphene oxide [12], organic crystalline solid [17] and metal organic framework (MOF) [15]. With the development of nanocarbon materials, carbon-based photoluminescent nanodots have gained a wide concern due to their superiority in chemical stability, biocompatibility and tunable photoluminescence in comparison with luminescent quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes. These excellent properties make them ideal materials in a wide range of applications fields such as bioimaging [20–22], biosensing [23], photodynamic therapy [21] and analytic purpose [24–28]. Therefore, developing a simple, highly selective and sensitive detect method of TNP by using CNDs is very fascinating.

Herein, we demonstrate the synthesis and performance of a novel, highly sensitive TNP nanosensing material based on photoluminescent CNDs synthesized by using spermine and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors. ( Scheme 1). The as-prepared blue–green photoluminescent CNDs have an excitation band at 360nm, which has a good spectral overlap with absorption band of TNP at 356nm (ε=1.048×104 cm−1 M−1), making TNP has a strong ability to shield the excitation light for CNDs, and thus a high efficiency of IFE occurs between TNP and CNDs. The fluorescence intensity of CNDs at 490nm is linearly quenched with the adding concentration of TNP. Comparison studies demonstrate that the sensing assay shows good selectivity to TNP, and other nitroaromatic explosives and common metal ions have no influence on the fluorescence emission of CNDs.

Spermine, m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and nitroaromatic chemicals, including phenol (PHE), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), methylbenzene (MB), 2,4,6-trinitromethylbenzene (TNT), 2-nitrotoluene (o-NT), 4-nitro-o-xylene (4-NOX), 4-nitrotoluene (p-NT), 2,6-dinitrotoluene (DNT), nitrobenzene (NB), 1,3-dinitrobenzene (DNB) are all from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl) and a Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer are adopted to adjust the ionic strength and control the acidity, respectively.

The UV absorption spectra of nitroaromatic chemicals are obtained from a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The elemental composition of CNDs is measured with an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The IR spectrum of CNDs is collected on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The TEM data of CNDs is performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The fluorescence spectra of CNDs are recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime of CNDs is measured with a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). The Raman spectrum of CNDs on the AgNPs solution is scanned through a LabRAM HR800 Laser confocal Raman spectrometer.

Spermine (50mg) and MPD (100mg) are simultaneously dissolved in 5.0mL water, and the mixture is then transferred into a 25mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 3h. After the autoclave is cooled down naturally, the resulting mixture is transferred to filter through a 0.45µm membrane and then dialyzed with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (500–1000 MWCO) for 24h to remove spermine and MPD molecules. The resulting products are dried by lyophilization to obtain CNDs powder, which is dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

The TEM image ( Fig. 1a) of CNDs clearly shows that the CNDs are well mono-dispersed. Their size distribution ranges from 1.0nm to 4.5nm (inset in Fig. 1a) with maximum population at about 2.75nm±1.75nm. The as-obtained produces are regarded as the amorphous CNDs because there are not observed obvious lattice fringes through high resolution TEM (HRTEM).

Owing to π–π* transition of the aromatic ring structure and n–π* transition of C–N, the as-prepared CNDs exhibit a very broad UV–vis absorption with two shoulder peaks at 293 and 342nm (Fig. 1b) [29]. Under the excitation of 320–400nm light beam, the as-prepared CNDs show excitation wavelength independent fluorescence emissions, and the maximum emission is located at 490nm with the maximum excitation at 360nm (Fig. 1b).

FT-IR and XPS measurements are further performed to explore the chemical composition and structure of the CNDs. The FT-IR spectrum ( Fig. 2a) of the as-prepared CNDs exhibits the characteristic absorption bands of N–H, C–H and C–O stretching vibrations at 3435cm−1, 2923cm−1 and 1074cm−1, respectively [19]. The peaks at 1633cm−1 and 1544cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic stretching [30]. Additionally, the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) analysis shows that C1s, N1s and O1s peaks are located about at 284.7, 398.7 and 530.7eV, respectively [31]. The result indicates that the as-synthesized CNDs are mainly composed of C, N as well as limited amount of O element with a C/N/O ratio of 0.741/0.171/0.088. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows three peaks at 284.5, 285.2 and 286.6eV, which are attributed to C–C, C–N and C–O, respectively [31]. Raman spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows that the D-band at 1373cm−1 is owing to sp3 carbons, while the G-band at 1587cm−1 is due to sp2 carbons [28]. Moreover, the absolute quantum yield of the as-prepared CNDs reaches 11.7%, which indicates that the CNDs act as fluorescence nanoprobes can be well applied for analysis purpose.
All the above results show that the amorphous blue–green photoluminescence CNDs are successfully synthesized and surrounded by some chemical groups on the surface, such as amino groups, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the as-prepared CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability is one particularly important property of nanomaterials, which could avoid false positive/negative results in chemical and biological sensing. The fluorescence intensity of the CNDs remains almost constant on a very broad pH range from 6.09 to 10.88 ( Fig. 3a). In the meanwhile, the CNDs are very stable in a salt solution. The fluorescence intensity of CNDs slightly changed even if in the medium of NaCl concentration as high as 4M (Fig. 3b), indicating that the CNDs can be very stable in a medium of comparatively high ionic strength. Considering that most CNDs reported are easily oxidized, we particularly investigate the antioxidant capacity of the proposed CNDs, and it is found that the as-prepared CNDs have very smart oxidation resistance even if in a medium of H2O2 higher than 0.3M (Fig. 3c). Note that the fluorescence intensity of CNDs shows no discernible decrease after 60min excitation at 365nm with a UV lamp (Fig. 3d). Therefore, the as-synthesized CNDs are very stable, especially in high salty medium and at high concentration of H2O2 solution, which is beneficial for TNP detection in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific binding of the as-prepared CNDs with TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CNDs show great importance in highly selective and sensitive detecting TNP. As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence of the CNDs can be effectively quenched in the presence of TNP. The fluorescence response (Fig. 4b) has a wide linear range of three orders of magnitude with the concentrations of TNP from 0.1 to 100μM with a limit of detection of 28nM. Importantly, in comparison to other TNP sensors with fluorescent probes (such as carbon dots, fluorescent molecules), the propose method own higher sensitivity and wider linear range [18,32,33]. Moreover, the synthesis of CNDs is simple and low-cost without the need of doping of rare earth or other materials [19].

Accompanied with the increase of TNP concentration, the blue–green fluorescence of the CNDs is gradually weakened, which supplied a colorimetric assay for TNP detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is analyzed by Stern−Volmer equation [12]:
(1)
I
0
I
=
K
SV
[ Q ] + 1
where I 0 and I are fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of the TNP, [Q] is the molar concentration of TNP and K SV is the quenching constant (M−1). In this work, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 0/I) of CNDs in the absence and presence of TNP is linear with the concentration of TNP with the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of K sv=1.066×105 M−1.

In this study, we find the fluorescence of CNDs can be efficiently quenched by TNP because of the strong IFE between them. TNP has strong characteristic absorption at 356nm (ε=1.048×104 cm−1 M−1) in the all nitroaromatics, and shows precise overlap with the excitation spectrum of CNDs ( Fig. 5a). Moreover, there is no changes of the lifetimes for CNDs before and after adding TNP (Fig. 5b), which further confirms the strong IFE occurring between CNDs and TNP.

The TNP-induced fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared CNDs is highly specific. As Fig. 6a shows, 100μM TNP and other nitroaromatic compounds (PHE, MB, NB, o-NT, p-NT, DNT, 4-NOX, TNT and DNB) are selected to study the fluorescence performance of the CNDs. Compared with the control group, the fluorescence intensity of CNDs has no change with other nitroaromatic explosives separately, indicating that the influence of other nitroaromatics is negligible. Similarly, nitroaromatic compounds have no influence on fluorescence intensity of CNDs in the presence of 100μM TNP as well.

In addition, the metal ions are also used to investigate the selectivity of TNP detection (Fig. 6b). All the metal ions have no influence on fluorescence intensity of CNDs in the presence and absence of TNP. Therefore, the highly selective property can be the basis for detecting TNP in the water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TNP in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method is applied in the detection of TNP in natural water samples by detecting recoveries after adding given amounts of TNP. As shown in the Table 1, the obtained recoveries are from 94.5% to 108.0% and 97.9% to 104.4% when standard TNP solutions (10, 25, 50μM) are added, indicating that the proposed fluorescence quenching method is promising for the detection of TNP in our daily life. Owing to the good stability and anti-interference ability of the CNDs, the recovery results reveal the high reproducibility and accuracy of this approach with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of each sample below 1.6%.

In this study, amorphous CNDs are synthesized successfully through a simple and rapid hydrothermal route. By strong IFE between the as-prepared CNDs and TNP, we develop a facile, highly selective and sensitive fluorescence method for TNP detection with the limit of detection of 28nM, identifying the concept that a high efficiency of the IFE requires a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of absorber and the excitation band of fluorescent probe. The as-prepared CNDs own great salt stability and antioxidant capacity, which supports the application in the complex water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors herein are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant no. 21535006).
